- Definition: Percent change in employment or labor-force participation from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage.
- Target interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage
- Population/context: Single mothers in the United States
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

Return valid JSON only with exactly this shape:
{
  "interpretation": "...",
  "point_estimate": <number>,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": <number>,
    "p25": <number>,
    "p50": <number>,
    "p75": <number>,
    "p95": <number>
  },
  "citations": ["..."],
  "reasoning_summary": "..."
}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the US w.r.t. net-of-tax wage
runs.reasoningSummary: Influenced by EITC studies showing moderate employment responses (0.1-0.5) for single mothers; medium-run effects likely higher than short-run; prior on low elasticities for participation due to childcare constraints, but positive wage incentives matter.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation":"Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the US w.r.t. net-of-tax wage","point_estimate":0.3,"quantiles":{"p05":0.05,"p25":0.15,"p50":0.3,"p75":0.6,"p95":1.2},"citations":["Eissa and Liebman 1996","Blau and Kahn 2007","Herbst 2010"],"reasoning_summary":"Influenced by EITC studies showing moderate employment responses (0.1-0.5) for single mothers; medium-run effects likely higher than short-run; prior on low elasticities for participation due to childcare constraints, but positive wage incentives matter."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the US w.r.t. net-of-tax wage
runs.reasoningSummary: Influenced by EITC expansions showing moderate employment boosts for single mothers; elasticities typically 0.1-0.7 in medium run, skewed positive due to income effects weaker than substitution for this group.
